name: Request Written Confirmation Before Hanging Up
description: Before saying goodbye, request a written confirmation of the agreement via email, in-account portal note, SMS, or mailed letter — and pin a specific arrival window. Verbal lock-in is necessary; written confirmation is the durable artifact most companies will honour without dispute.

context.when_to_use: Any call that produced a commitment, change, refund, credit, schedule, or set of conditions. ALWAYS request written confirmation, even when the rep says 'it's not necessary' — that's exactly when it's most necessary.
preconditions: Terms have already been locked verbally (see `lock-in-terms-verbally`).
The user has a working email address, portal login, or mailing address on file.
The rep has acknowledged the agreement exists in their system.

principles: Email > portal > SMS > mailed letter, in that order. Email is searchable, timestamped, forwardable, and unambiguous.
Ask for the written confirmation BEFORE you accept the agreement, not after. 'I'll need that in writing — can you send it before we hang up?' is leverage; asking afterwards is begging.
Pin an arrival window. 'You'll get it' is a non-commitment. 'Within 24 hours' is a checkable claim.
Insist that the written version include the same specifics: amount, date, duration, conditions, rollback rules, reference number, and rep ID.
If the company 'cannot send' written confirmation, that itself is a written-able fact — get them to email saying so. Refusal-to-confirm-in-writing is a documentable warning sign.

phrases.opener: Before we finish, could you send me a written confirmation of what we just agreed to? Email is ideal — and ideally within the next 24 hours so I can reference it if anything looks different on the next bill.
phrases.format_preference: What format do you usually send those in — email, an in-account notification, or mailed letter? Email is best for me if it's an option.
phrases.content_spec: Could you make sure the email includes the new [amount / date / credit / conditions] and the reference number [R]? That way I can cross-check it against my notes.
phrases.arrival_window_pin: When should I expect it to land — same day, 24 hours, 3 business days? I'll set a reminder so I follow up if I don't see it.
phrases.no_email_workaround: If you can't send email directly, can you add a detailed note to my account that I can screenshot from the portal? Or is there a chat transcript that gets emailed?
phrases.refusal_documentation: Just so I'm clear — your company doesn't provide written confirmation of [type of change]? I'd like to note that, and could you tell me where that policy is published?
phrases.graceful_close_success: Perfect, I'll watch my inbox for the email from [domain] within [window] — referencing case [R]. Thanks for sending that.
phrases.graceful_close_failure: I'd like to escalate this — I'm not comfortable closing on a change with no paper trail. Could you get me a supervisor before we hang up?

tactics.name: Ask for written confirmation as part of the close, not after it
tactics.script: Use the `opener` phrase. Bundling the written request with the close makes it a routine step rather than an exceptional ask.

tactics.name: Pin the format
tactics.when: Rep agrees to send something written.
tactics.script: Use the `format_preference` phrase. Steer toward email; accept portal note as a strong second; treat 'we'll mail you a letter' as a yellow flag (slow, often gets lost).

tactics.name: Specify the content
tactics.when: Rep agrees to send a confirmation.
tactics.script: Use the `content_spec` phrase. Generic confirmation emails ('thank you for being a customer') are useless. Force the rep to commit to including the specifics.

tactics.name: Pin the arrival window
tactics.when: After format and content are agreed.
tactics.script: Use the `arrival_window_pin` phrase. The window becomes the trigger for the follow-up — if nothing arrives, you have grounds to call back referencing this exact commitment.

tactics.name: Work around 'we can't email'
tactics.when: Rep claims they cannot send email confirmation.
tactics.script: Use the `no_email_workaround` phrase. Portal note, SMS, chat transcript, mailed letter — get SOMETHING in writing. If literally nothing is possible, escalate.

tactics.name: Document the refusal
tactics.when: Company genuinely refuses any written confirmation.
tactics.script: Use the `refusal_documentation` phrase. Capture the rep stating the policy verbally so the refusal itself is on the recording. Then escalate.

boundaries: Do NOT accept 'we already sent it' without checking inbox during the call when feasible — confirmations sometimes go to spam, wrong address, or were never actually sent.
Do NOT settle for a marketing email that vaguely mentions 'recent account changes'. Confirmation = the SPECIFIC change, with the SPECIFIC numbers.
Do NOT close the call without an expected arrival window. 'Eventually' = never.
Do NOT email-bomb the rep with follow-up requests during the same call — one clean ask + content spec + window is enough.
success_signals: Rep states the email will arrive within a specific window (e.g. 'within the next 2 hours').
Rep reads back the email address on file to verify delivery.
Rep proactively says 'I'm including the reference number and the new rate in the email'.
Confirmation arrives during the call itself — gold standard.
failure_signals: Rep says 'you'll see it on your next bill' as a substitute for written confirmation.
Rep is vague on arrival window ('soon', 'when the system updates').
Rep refuses to confirm the email will include specific terms.
Company policy reportedly bans written confirmation for this type of change — major red flag, escalate.

exit_strategy.on_success: Log the expected arrival window, format, and sender domain. Calendar a check at the END of the window. If nothing arrives, call back referencing the prior call's identifiers and quote the rep's commitment to send.
exit_strategy.on_failure: Escalate before closing. If the company has no written-confirmation capability, document that refusal, then weigh whether the agreement is worth proceeding with — sometimes the answer is no.
follow_ups: When the email arrives, cross-check every line item against the verbal lock-in transcript.
If terms differ, the more favourable version (to the user) is usually defensible — but flag the discrepancy in writing immediately.

required_user_info: Email address and/or portal login the company has on file
Tolerance for follow-up if written confirmation fails to arrive
Decision threshold for proceeding without written confirmation (default: do not proceed)
